Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines standard sources: a local man page (`rar.1`) and a tarball from the official RAR website (`https://www.rarlab.com/rar/rarlinux-x64-${__pkgver}.tar.gz`). The `sha512sums` array provides fixed checksums for both files, which `makepkg` will verify upon download. No command substitutions, function calls, or executable logic appear in the global scope or within `pkgver()` (which is absent, so the top-level `pkgver` is used directly). The `source` array does not include any `git+` URLs or other VCS sources that could trigger unexpected fetch logic. There is no network access via `curl`, `wget`, `eval`, or other commands during sourcing. The environment only downloads declared sources and validates them via checksums, which is standard and safe behavior for `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare`.
</details>
<summary>
No malicious code in sourcing or source fetch
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a standard Arch Linux package for the rar utility, maintained by known contributors. It fetches the source from the official RARLab website (https://www.rarlab.com) using a versioned tarball URL, which is appropriate and expected. The sha512 checksum is provided for integrity verification, indicating good packaging hygiene.

The package installs standard binaries (rar, unrar), a man page, a license file, a configuration file, and a default SFX module to standard system locations. All operations are confined to the package directory (${pkgdir}) and follow typical Arch packaging practices. The only network destination is the official upstream, and no dynamic code execution, network exfiltration, or unexpected system modifications occur.

No obfuscated code, dangerous commands (like curl|bash), or suspicious file operations are present. The provides=('unrar') directive is common for packages that aim to replace unrar, and the conflicts array prevents installation conflicts. The package is SAFE.
</details>
<summary>
Standard package with official source and proper checksums.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for acknow.txt:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file, "acknow.txt", contains acknowledgments for the use of various third-party algorithms and code in the RAR software. It cites academic papers, public domain implementations (e.g., PPMII, rangecoder, AES, SHA-1), and algorithms from Intel and individual contributors. All referenced external assets—such as Intel's Slicing-by-8 CRC32 implementation and BLAKE2sp—are appropriately attributed and link to legitimate, public sources.

There is no executable code or suspicious commands in this file. It serves only as a documentation file listing attributions and licensing notices. No network activity, file operations, or system calls are present. References to URLs are for informational purposes and do not imply automatic execution or download. The use of public domain or BSD-licensed code, when properly attributed, is standard practice in software distribution.

The content poses no security risk and aligns with typical open-source and software packaging disclosure norms. There are no signs of obfuscation, malicious payloads, or unauthorized data exfiltration.
</details>
<summary>
Benign acknowledgment file with no security issues</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for rar.txt:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file is a user manual (rar.txt) for the RAR 7.22 console application. It contains detailed documentation on RAR commands, switches, configuration, and usage instructions. The content is purely descriptive text with no executable code, scripts, or commands that could pose a security risk. There are no instances of suspicious patterns such as curl, wget, eval, base64, exec, or any network-related operations. The file does not contain any instructions for downloading, executing, or exfiltrating data. It is consistent with standard documentation typically included in software packages, particularly for command-line archiving tools. The presence of switches like -si (standard input) or -so (standard output) are legitimate features of the RAR tool and do not constitute a security threat in this context.
</details>
<summary>
Documentation only, no malicious content detected.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[3/9] Reviewing rar.1, src/rar/license.txt, src/rar/rarfiles.lst, src/rar/makefile, src/rar/whatsnew.txt, src/rar/order.htm...

LLM auditresponse for rarfiles.lst:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file `rarfiles.lst` is a configuration file intended for use with the RAR archiver to define the order in which file types are added to a solid archive. It consists of comments (lines starting with ';') and a list of file patterns (using wildcards and specific filenames). The file contains no executable code, scripts, or system commands. It does not perform any network operations, file modifications outside its purpose, or include obfuscated content.

All entries are standard file extensions and patterns related to common document, media, source code, and archive formats. The use of `$default` is documented within the comments as a placeholder for unmatched files. The structure and content align with legitimate RAR configuration practices. There are no signs of malicious logic, data exfiltration attempts, or unauthorized operations.

This file serves a purely declarative role and poses no security threat when placed in the expected context (same directory as RAR.EXE). It cannot execute code or initiate network connections on its own.
</details>
<summary>
Configuration file with no executable or malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[4/9] Reviewing rar.1, src/rar/license.txt, src/rar/makefile, src/rar/whatsnew.txt, src/rar/order.htm...

LLM auditresponse for license.txt:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file is a license agreement (EULA) for the RAR/WinRAR software distributed by win.rar GmbH. It outlines standard licensing terms, including permitted usage, distribution rules for the trial version, warranty disclaimers, and restrictions on reverse engineering. The content does not contain any executable code, scripts, URLs, or commands—only plain legal text describing user rights and obligations.

There are no signs of malicious content, obfuscation, network activity, or system manipulation. The mention in section 4 of possible web page loading for registration reminders is within normal software behavior and does not imply unauthorized data exfiltration, as only standard HTTP parameters are transmitted. This file poses no security risk as it is purely informational and part of legitimate software documentation.

The file aligns with the official licensing practices of proprietary software distributed via AUR or other package repositories. No supply chain tampering indicators are present.
</details>
<summary>
Standard RAR license agreement; no malicious content</summary>
</security_assessment>

[5/9] Reviewing rar.1, src/rar/makefile, src/rar/whatsnew.txt, src/rar/order.htm...
+ Reviewed src/rar/license.txt. Status: SAFE -- Standard RAR license agreement; no malicious content
LLM auditresponse for whatsnew.txt:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file, `whatsnew.txt`, is a plaintext changelog for the RAR software, detailing bug fixes, new features, and improvements across multiple versions. It contains no executable code, scripts, or directives that could be interpreted as malicious. The content describes changes such as enhanced security measures (e.g., fixing out-of-bounds writes, preventing file extraction outside destination directories), improved compression algorithms, and configuration file path updates—all typical of legitimate software maintenance.

There are no signs of obfuscation, unauthorized network activity, or payload delivery mechanisms. Mentions of security fixes (e.g., CVE-like reports from researchers) further indicate responsible development practices. The file serves purely informational purposes and aligns with standard release notes distributed with software packages, including those in the AUR.

Therefore, this file poses no security risk.
</details>
<summary>
Changelog with no executable or malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[6/9] Reviewing rar.1, src/rar/makefile, src/rar/order.htm...
+ Reviewed src/rar/whatsnew.txt. Status: SAFE -- Changelog with no executable or malicious content
LLM auditresponse for order.htm:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `order.htm` is a static HTML document providing instructions on how to purchase and register WinRAR and RAR licenses. It contains embedded JavaScript functions (`px` and `mx`) designed to obfuscate an email address (`sales@win-razyr.com`) from scrapers, a common and benign practice. The script works by replacing the substring "zy" with "y", suggesting a simple character substitution cipher, potentially intended to avoid direct harvesting of the email by bots. The actual rendered email address, however, resolves to a domain (`win-razyr.com`) that appears altered—note the unusual name and the presence of an 'x' in 'xwin-rar.com' in the fallback text. However, this domain is presented as a contact method within a licensing document, and the file overall does not execute any payloads, perform network requests, or access sensitive local files. All links point to the legitimate domain `rarlab.com`, which is the official site for WinRAR. The obfuscation is limited to content display and does not hide malicious commands or remote downloads.
</details>
<summary>
Benign HTML with email obfuscation</summary>
</security_assessment>

[7/9] Reviewing rar.1, src/rar/makefile...
+ Reviewed src/rar/order.htm. Status: SAFE -- Benign HTML with email obfuscation
LLM auditresponse for rar.1:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file is a Unix man page (specifically, `rar.1`) documenting the usage, commands, and switches for the `rar` command-line utility. It is written in `nroff`/troff format, which is standard for Unix manual pages. 

An analysis of the content reveals no evidence of malicious code, suspicious network operations, or potentially dangerous commands such as `curl`, `wget`, `eval`, `base64`, or `exec`. The file exclusively contains documentation: explanations of commands (`a`, `x`, `t`, etc.), switches (like `-v`, `-p`, `-rr`), and usage examples. It also describes configuration files, environment variables, and exit codes in a manner consistent with standard software documentation.

The presence of standard macro definitions (`.de`, `.ds`, `.tr`, etc.) is appropriate for a man page and does not indicate obfuscation. All operations described are within the expected functionality of the `rar` archiver. There is no code that modifies system settings, downloads external content, or handles sensitive data in an unsafe way.

This file is a legitimate part of the `rar` package’s documentation and poses no security risk.
</details>
<summary>
Documentation file for rar command, no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[8/9] Reviewing src/rar/makefile...
+ Reviewed rar.1. Status: SAFE -- Documentation file for rar command, no malicious content
LLM auditresponse for makefile:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided `makefile` performs standard installation tasks for the RAR utility, including creating directories and copying binaries and configuration files to system locations. It installs `rar` and `unrar` binaries to `$(PREFIX)/bin` (which defaults to `/usr/local/bin`), copies `rarfiles.lst` to `/etc`, and places `default.sfx` in `$(PREFIX)/lib`. All operations use simple `cp` and `mkdir` commands with predictable, hardcoded paths.

There are no signs of malicious activity such as network operations (e.g., `curl`, `wget`), obfuscated commands (e.g., `base64`, `eval`), or unexpected file manipulations. The actions are consistent with typical package installation procedures for RAR on Unix-like systems. While writing directly to `/etc` and `/usr/local` could be a privilege escalation concern if misused, in this context it reflects expected behavior for a manually installed binary package, not an attack.

No `unsafe patterns` were detected in the head, tail, or grep output of the file.
</details>
<summary>
Standard installation script, no malicious behavior detected</summary>
</security_assessment>

[9/9] Reviewing ...
+ Reviewed src/rar/makefile. Status: SAFE -- Standard installation script, no malicious behavior detected

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a standard source download from the official RAR website (`https://www.rarlab.com`) using a direct HTTPS link to a version-specific tarball. The `sha512sums` array provides valid checksums for both the local `rar.1` manpage and the remote tarball, ensuring integrity verification during source retrieval. No command substitution, unescaped interpolation, or executable logic appears in the global scope or within `pkgver()`, which is absent — meaning the hardcoded `pkgver` is used directly. The `source` array contains no git+ protocols, dynamic URLs, or references to untrusted domains.

Fetching the sources via `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` will securely download the specified tarball and local file without executing any build or prepare functions. The commands run during this phase are limited to sourcing the PKGBUILD (which contains only declarative metadata and safe assignments) and verifying checksums. There is no evidence of malicious payloads, data exfiltration attempts, or remote code execution during the sourcing or source-fetching stages.

All practices shown are consistent with standard, safe AUR packaging. The use of a hardcoded version and checksummed download from the vendor's official site reduces supply-chain risk. No top-level code execution or dangerous substitutions occur when sourcing the file.
</details>
<summary>
Safe source retrieval with verified HTTPS download.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Safe source retrieval with verified HTTPS download.[/]

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD fetches the RAR source archive from the official upstream domain (rarlab.com) over HTTPS, which is a trusted and appropriate source for this software. The checksums (sha512sums) are provided and match the expected values for the declared version, ensuring integrity. The build process consists solely of installing pre-compiled binaries (rar, unrar, default.sfx) and documentation files (LICENSE, rar.1, rarfiles.lst) into standard system directories, which is typical for binary-distributed packages.

There are no signs of obfuscated code, unauthorized network requests, or execution of downloaded content. The package does not run any scripts outside the official source, nor does it modify system files beyond what is expected. The use of SKIP for checksums is not present here—actual sha512sums are used, enhancing trust. All operations stay within the package build environment and follow standard Arch Linux packaging practices.

Although the package installs binaries without compiling from source (as expected for a closed-source tool), this is documented behavior for RAR, which is proprietary. No suspicious commands such as curl|bash, eval, or base64 decoding appear in the script. The maintainer metadata and source URL are consistent with the official RAR distribution.
</details>
<summary>
Standard binary package from official source, no malicious code</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for readme.txt:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file "readme.txt" is a plain text document containing informational content about the RAR archiver's features, including its compression algorithm, solid archive functionality, SFX archive support, multi-volume archives, and additional services like encryption and archive repair.

There is no executable code, scripts, or system commands present in the file. The content is entirely descriptive and contains only text outlining the capabilities of the RAR software. It does not perform any file operations, network requests, or system modifications.

The use of the HTML entity ' for an apostrophe is benign and properly formatted. Since the file contains no logic, automation, or binary content, and is consistent with standard documentation for software packages, it poses no security risk.
</details>
<summary>

</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for makefile:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided makefile performs standard installation tasks for the RAR utility, including copying executables (`rar`, `unrar`) to `$(PREFIX)/bin`, installing a configuration file (`rarfiles.lst`) to `/etc`, and placing the default SFX module (`default.sfx`) in `$(PREFIX)/lib`. All operations use standard Unix commands (`mkdir`, `cp`) and target typical system directories with predictable paths. The `PREFIX` is set to `/usr/local`, which is a conventional installation prefix for locally built software.

There are no signs of malicious activity such as network operations (e.g., `curl`, `wget`), obfuscated commands, data exfiltration, or execution of downloaded content. The operations are consistent with the expected behavior of a legitimate software installation script for a package like `rar`. While writing directly to `/etc` and `/usr/local` requires privilege, this is normal for installation scripts and not inherently malicious.

The absence of suspicious patterns (e.g., `eval`, `base64`, `exec`, shell pipelines with decoded content) and the transparency of the operations support a SAFE assessment. This appears to be a standard, non-malicious makefile used in a build or install process.
</details>
<summary>
Standard installation script, no malicious activity detected
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/9] Reviewing rar.1, src/rar/rarfiles.lst, src/rar/license.txt, src/rar/rar.txt, src/rar/order.htm, src/rar/acknow.txt, src/rar/whatsnew.txt...
+ Reviewed src/rar/makefile. Status: SAFE -- Standard installation script, no malicious activity detected
LLM auditresponse for rarfiles.lst:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `rarfiles.lst` is a configuration file used by the RAR archiving utility to define the order in which files are processed during solid archive creation. It contains file extensions and wildcards grouped by type, along with comments explaining its purpose and usage. The content is consistent with standard RAR behavior, prioritizing text and source files early in the archive for better deduplication and compression efficiency, while placing larger binary and multimedia files later.

There is no executable code, network activity, or file system manipulation present. The file does not perform any actions on its own and is purely declarative. It contains no obfuscated content, suspicious commands, or attempts to exfiltrate data. Lines beginning with semicolons are comments, as documented, and are ignored by the application. The `$default` entry is a documented RAR feature for unmatched files.

This file is a legitimate part of the RAR package and poses no security risk. Its purpose is transparent and aligns with the expected functionality of archive optimization.
</details>
<summary>
Configuration file for RAR archive ordering, no malicious content</summary>
</security_assessment>

[3/9] Reviewing rar.1, src/rar/license.txt, src/rar/rar.txt, src/rar/order.htm, src/rar/acknow.txt, src/rar/whatsnew.txt...
+ Reviewed src/rar/rarfiles.lst. Status: SAFE -- Configuration file for RAR archive ordering, no malicious content
LLM auditresponse for acknow.txt:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `acknow.txt` contains acknowledgments for third-party libraries, algorithms, and individuals who contributed to the functionality of the RAR software. It references well-known cryptographic and compression implementations such as Intel's Slicing-by-8 CRC32 algorithm, AES and SHA-1 code from public domain sources, BLAKE2sp, and compression algorithms by Dmitry Shkarin and others. All cited sources are reputable and relevant to the expected functionality of a data compression and archiving tool.

There is no executable code, no network activity, no file manipulation instructions, and no obfuscated content in this file. The mentions of URLs are for informational purposes only — to credit upstream authors and license terms — and do not involve automatic downloads or execution. The content aligns entirely with standard software attribution practices and poses no security risk.

This file is purely informational and consistent with expected packaging and licensing documentation in open-source or distributable software. No malicious or suspicious behavior is present.
</details>
<summary>
Purely informational; no code or malicious content

data.reasoning: The disagreement centers entirely on src/rar/order.htm, an upstream-distributed HTML documentation file about purchasing WinRAR licenses. The 'obfuscation' is a well-known, benign anti-spam email obfuscation technique used by RAR Labs for decades: 'win-razyr.com' decodes to 'win-rar.com' after the px/mx functions strip the 'zy' character pair, and 'xwin-rar.com' decodes to 'win-rar.com' after removing 'x'. These are NOT typo-squatted phishing domains — they are the legitimate official domain with deliberate junk characters inserted to defeat email-harvesting spambots. The deepseek model correctly identified this as benign email obfuscation. More importantly, order.htm is a static documentation file that is never executed or referenced by any PKGBUILD function or .install script; it is installed as inert documentation and poses zero supply-chain risk to makepkg. Even if the email were genuinely malicious, a static HTML file that the user would have to manually open and act upon does not constitute package-level unsafety.
data.disagreement_analysis: qwen3-235b misinterpreted RAR Labs' long-standing anti-spam email obfuscation (inserting 'zy'/'x' junk chars decoded by JS) as typo-squatting/phishing. It failed to actually apply the decode logic correctly — the decoded address is the legitimate sales@win-rar.com. It also failed to weigh that order.htm is inert documentation never executed during build/install. deepseek correctly decoded the obfuscation and recognized it as benign.
false_positives: qwen3-235b's order.htm 'phishing' finding: the px/mx JS decodes 'win-razyr.com' -> 'win-rar.com' and 'xwin-rar.com' -> 'win-rar.com', which is the legitimate vendor domain. This is standard anti-spam obfuscation, not typo-squatting. Additionally, the file is never executed by makepkg/install hooks, so it carries no package-level risk regardless.
data.coverage_notes: Both models reviewed identical file sets (10 files, skipped the binary tarball as expected). No coverage gap exists.
learnings: Models should fully apply/execute string-transformation logic before judging obfuscation as malicious. qwen flagged 'win-razyr.com' as a typo-squat without completing the decode that yields the legitimate 'win-rar.com'.

The threat model for AUR packages must be reinforced: static documentation files (HTML, txt, man pages) that are NOT referenced by PKGBUILD build/package/prepare/check functions or .install scripts cannot constitute package-level unsafety. Phishing content in inert docs should at most be a low-severity informational note, never an UNSAFE verdict.

Anti-spam email obfuscation (inserting junk characters decoded by JS, HTML entities like @) is an extremely common benign pattern and should be in a known-benign-pattern catalog to prevent false positives.
Severity calibration: the audit tool should distinguish 'executes during makepkg' (true supply-chain risk) from 'content a human might read' (out of scope), and gate UNSAFE verdicts on the former.
